topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammering started early, around seven in the morning, and it grated on my nerves like sandpaper on glass. I watched from my kitchen window as Mr. Henderson, my neighbor across the narrow strip of lawn, wrestled with the post-hole. He was loud, clumsy, and seemed to have an unlimited supply of enthusiasm for rearranging my property lines. When he finally paused, wiping sweat from his brow with the back of a dusty hand, he called out, "Just a little further on this side, then." I gripped the edge of the counter until my knuckles turned white. I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a hot flush creeping up my neck. I wanted to shout, to demand he stop, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a tight, brittle silence.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the length of my driveway, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles vibrating in my cheeks. Every time Mr. Henderson approached, offering a cheerful, if overly familiar, greeting, I would force a smile that felt like cracking plaster. "Morning, Tom!" he boomed, his voice carrying easily over the quiet street. I kept my gaze fixed on the freshly painted trim of my own house, refusing to meet his eyes. When he pointed toward the property line, his gesture seemed to mock my composure. A low, simmering anger began to bubble beneath my skin, a heavy, unpleasant pressure. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to regulate the sudden tremor in my hands before I could snap something sharp.

[story 3]

It was the sheer audacity of the timing that truly set me on edge. The construction noise began precisely when I was trying to enjoy my morning coffee, a perfect, peaceful moment shattered by the rhythmic thud of wood against earth. I stood by the mailbox, arms crossed so tightly my shoulders ached, watching him measure the distance with a tape measure. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of unshakeable certainty, as if he were the rightful sovereign of the boundary. I felt a sudden, sharp coldness settle in my stomach, a defensive tightening. "I really think," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally flat, "that we should discuss this arrangement more carefully." The tone I used was far colder than I intended, a deliberate withdrawal of warmth.
